evidence_refs.summary: 943010b Derived from BD: BD-023

core.when: When implementing order cancellation logic in backtesting
core.action: Orders persist until explicitly filled or cancelled — default CancelPolicy is NeverCancel; if implementing time-based expiration, clearly document and handle order non-execution scenarios

consequence.description: Backtests assuming orders never expire may show execution rates higher than live trading where orders can expire; strategies relying on unfilled orders behaving differently need explicit expiration handling

evidence_refs.summary: 943010b Derived from BD: BD-024

core.when: When implementing history lookback logic for daily-frequency backtesting
core.action: Verify that 40 trading days is sufficient for the strategy's required lookback period; for strategies needing 50+ days of daily data, explicitly configure a longer window

consequence.description: Strategies requiring lookbacks beyond 40 days will experience data truncation or cache misses, causing incorrect technical indicator calculations and potentially signaling trades at wrong prices

evidence_refs.summary: 943010b Derived from BD: BD-026

core.when: When calculating annualized performance metrics (Sharpe ratio, returns, volatility)
core.action: Use TRADING_DAYS_IN_YEAR=250 for US equity annualization, which accounts for weekends and market holidays; do not use calendar days (365) or exact trading days (252) without explicit justification

consequence.description: Using 365 calendar days underestimates annualized returns by ~31%; using 252 exact days inflates Sharpe by ~1.5%; both cause strategy selection errors and investor miscommunication about expected performance

evidence_refs.summary: 943010b Derived from BD: BD-029

core.when: When converting minute-frequency returns to annualized metrics
core.action: Use TRADING_HOURS_IN_DAY=6.5 for minute annualization (US equity 9:30 AM - 4:00 PM EST with buffer); exclude pre/post-market minutes from standard annualization unless explicitly modeling extended hours

consequence.description: Converting minute returns with incorrect hourly assumption distorts annualized volatility estimates; strategies appear more or less risky than they actually are, leading to inappropriate position sizing and risk management

evidence_refs.summary: 943010b Derived from BD: BD-030

core.when: When implementing OLMAR or similar mean-reversion portfolio strategies
core.action: Verify OLMAR parameters (eps=1, window_length=5) match the strategy's risk tolerance and market regime; these defaults are tuned for specific market conditions and may require adjustment for different asset classes or time periods

consequence.description: Using OLMAR defaults without tuning produces suboptimal portfolios; eps=1 allows only 100% deviation from uniform allocation which may be too restrictive for volatile markets or too aggressive for stable ones

core.when: When implementing OLMAR portfolio optimization weight normalization
core.action: Apply simplex projection to verify portfolio weights remain non-negative and sum to exactly 1.0 — do not use simple truncation or scaling methods that fail to satisfy both constraints simultaneously

consequence.description: Using simple truncation instead of simplex projection produces invalid portfolio allocations where weights either exceed budget or fail to sum to 1.0, causing backtest failures or misleading equity curves

evidence_refs.summary: 943010b Derived from BD: BD-035

core.when: When configuring PerDollar commission model for backtesting
core.action: Verify that $0.0015 per dollar (15 bps) matches actual broker fee structure — typical retail brokers use per-share commissions ($0.005-0.01 per share), not percentage-of-assets fees

consequence.description: Using percentage-of-assets commission (15 bps) underestimates costs for strategies with small capital turnover and overestimates for high-turnover strategies, distorting strategy profitability estimates

evidence_refs.summary: 943010b Derived from BD: BD-038

core.action: Verify that $0.85 per contract matches actual futures broker fees — exchange and instrument-specific fees vary significantly; adjust for high-frequency strategies where commission dominates returns

evidence_refs.summary: 943010b Derived from BD: BD-039

core.when: When implementing order execution price rounding logic
core.action: Apply 0.95 threshold for asymmetric rounding direction — when fractional part >= 0.95, round against trader (down for buys, up for sells) to model conservative market maker behavior

consequence.description: Using symmetric midpoint rounding (0.5 threshold) creates artificially optimistic execution assumptions, causing backtest returns to exceed what would be achieved in live trading with realistic slippage

evidence_refs.summary: 943010b Derived from BD: BD-040

core.when: When using or refactoring MACD factor implementation
core.action: Change MACD parameters (fast=12, slow=26, signal=9) without validation — these are non-adaptive and assume stationary statistical properties over the signal window; modifying them alters the indicator's sensitivity to market cycles

consequence.description: Changing MACD periods from 12/26/9 to different values changes signal timing and crossover frequency, potentially turning a profitable strategy into a losing one without proper re-optimization and walk-forward validation

evidence_refs.summary: 943010b Derived from BD: BD-046

core.when: When applying RollingPearson correlation to non-linear or non-normal return relationships
core.action: Verify that the relationship between X and Y is approximately linear and that return distributions are approximately normal before using RollingPearson; for non-linear dependencies, use RollingSpearman instead

consequence.description: Pearson correlation assumes linear relationships; applying it to non-linear dependencies (e.g., quadratic, exponential) produces near-zero correlation for strongly related pairs, causing factor models to miss true relationships and produce invalid signals

evidence_refs.summary: 943010b Derived from BD: BD-047

core.when: When applying RollingLinearRegression to heteroscedastic or autocorrelated financial time series
core.action: Use RollingLinearRegression for financial returns with homoscedastic, uncorrelated errors only — heteroscedasticity or autocorrelation invalidates standard errors and produces unreliable beta estimates and statistical significance metrics

consequence.description: OLS standard errors computed by scipy.stats.linregress become unreliable when applied to heteroscedastic return series (common during volatile periods), producing statistically significant betas that are actually noise and leading to wrong risk estimates

evidence_refs.summary: 943010b Derived from BD: BD-049

core.when: When applying winsorize to asymmetric factor distributions
core.action: Verify that winsorization percentile thresholds match expected tail distributions — symmetric winsorization may not suit asymmetric factors; adjust lower and upper percentiles independently for skewed distributions

consequence.description: Symmetric winsorization at (1%, 99%) clips both tails equally, which over-clips the shorter tail and under-clips the longer tail in asymmetric distributions, distorting factor rankings and causing suboptimal portfolio construction

evidence_refs.summary: 943010b Derived from BD: BD-055

core.when: When using VolumeShareSlippage in markets with non-stationary volume patterns
core.action: Recalibrate the volume impact coefficient periodically — VolumeShareSlippage assumes stationary volume patterns; the quadratic impact formula breaks down when ADV changes significantly (e.g., during earnings, crisis periods)

consequence.description: Using stale VolumeShareSlippage calibration causes systematic underestimation of trading costs when market liquidity drops or overestimation when liquidity increases, leading to backtest results that do not match live execution costs

evidence_refs.summary: 943010b Derived from BD: BD-056

core.when: When applying FixedBasisPointsSlippage to large block trades or time-varying liquidity
core.action: Use FixedBasisPointsSlippage only for small-to-medium orders with constant market impact — for block trades exceeding volume cap or intraday periods with varying liquidity, the fixed model systematically underestimates impact

consequence.description: FixedBasisPointsSlippage underestimates market impact for large block orders because the quadratic relationship between order size and impact is not captured, causing backtested execution costs to appear lower than actual live trading costs

evidence_refs.summary: 943010b Derived from BD: BD-057

core.when: When configuring EWMA factors in pipeline calculations
core.action: Explicitly specify warm-up period sufficient for EWMA convergence (typically 3-5x the decay parameter); EWMA has infinite theoretical memory with exponentially decaying weights that never fully converge, requiring explicit warm-up to avoid factor contamination from early observations

consequence.description: Insufficient warm-up causes EWMA factors to be contaminated by early observations that may reflect a different market regime, leading to factor values that don't represent current market conditions and degraded strategy signals

consequence.description: Price scaling to uint32 loses precision below 0.001 units and causes silent overflow for assets priced above ~$4 million, leading to execution errors or system failures when backtesting high-priced securities or cryptocurrencies with fine price granularity
